learned_rules.trigger: When extracting linear or area measurements from any drawing, before applying a scale factor
learned_rules.action: Identify and explicitly record the drawing scale annotation (e.g. '1:100', '1:50') from the title block or nearest scale bar. Cross-check by measuring a known reference element (e.g. a door leaf assumed 900mm, a standard room module, or a north arrow bar scale). If the measured reference does not match the expected real-world dimension within ±5%, flag a scale conflict and halt extraction pending human confirmation.
learned_rules.reason: Because a scale misread of 1:50 vs 1:100 doubles every linear measurement on the sheet, producing systematic overcount errors across all pipe runs, cable routes, ductwork, and containment. A single undetected scale error contaminates the entire drawing take-off.
learned_rules.example_before: AI reads scale annotation as 1:50 and measures pipe run as 150m.
learned_rules.example_after: AI reads scale annotation as 1:100, cross-checks against a reference dimension on the same sheet, confirms scale, and correctly measures pipe run as 85m.

learned_rules.trigger: When the extracted quantity for a linear service (pipe, duct, cable, containment) is suspiciously round or notably high relative to the visible drawing extent
learned_rules.action: Flag the item with a LOW_CONFIDENCE warning and note the specific scale used. Request estimator verification before accepting the quantity into the bill.
learned_rules.reason: Because round numbers (e.g. exactly 150m) can be a symptom of a scaling error where the measured value has been inadvertently doubled or halved by a factor of 2 — the most common scale ratio confusion (1:50/1:100, 1:100/1:200, 1:25/1:50).
learned_rules.example_before: AI outputs qty: 150m with confidence 72 and no scale audit trail recorded.
learned_rules.example_after: AI outputs qty: 150m but flags: 'SCALE_AUDIT_REQUIRED — measured at 1:50, cross-check pending. Confidence reduced to 45 until scale confirmed.'

learned_rules.trigger: When a scale error is confirmed on any one item within a drawing package or drawing sheet
learned_rules.action: Immediately apply a blanket review flag to ALL other items extracted from the same drawing reference or sheet number. Recalculate all affected quantities using the corrected scale factor before re-presenting to the estimator.
learned_rules.reason: Because a scale misread is a sheet-level error, not an item-level error. Every measurement taken from that sheet will carry the same multiplier error. Correcting only the reported item leaves all other items silently wrong.
learned_rules.example_before: Estimator corrects INS-001 from 150m to 85m. AI updates only INS-001 and leaves CHW-003 (50m), HWS-007 (30m) and EX-DUCT-002 (20m) from the same drawing sheet uncorrected at their doubled values.
learned_rules.example_after: Estimator corrects INS-001 from 150m to 85m. AI applies correction factor (85/150 = 0.567) to all items from the same drawing sheet, flags them for estimator sign-off: CHW-003 revised to ~28m, HWS-007 revised to ~17m, EX-DUCT-002 revised to ~11m.

learned_rules.trigger: When processing a drawing set containing multiple sheets, before beginning take-off
learned_rules.action: Build and record a scale register: log each drawing number, its stated scale, and the result of a reference-dimension cross-check. Where multiple sheets show different scales, apply per-sheet scale factors and never assume a uniform scale across the set.
learned_rules.reason: Because multi-discipline or multi-zone drawing packages frequently contain sheets at different scales (e.g. site plan at 1:200, floor plan at 1:100, detail at 1:20). Assuming a single scale across all sheets is a primary source of systematic overcount or undercount errors.
learned_rules.example_before: AI applies 1:100 uniformly across all sheets. Detail sheet is actually 1:20, so a 200mm pipe spool is extracted as 1000mm (1m) instead of 200mm.
learned_rules.example_after: AI records scale register: Drawing M-101: 1:100 (verified), Drawing M-201: 1:50 (verified), Drawing M-301 Detail: 1:20 (verified). Each take-off measurement references its own sheet scale.
